questions.question: Why do many distributed systems designate a single leader?
options: Leaders are cheaper to run
Having one node responsible for a task gives a single decision point, avoiding the conflicts and ordering problems of peer negotiation
It's required by TCP
Leaders never fail
questions.explanation: A single leader provides one place where ordering and decisions happen, sidestepping a whole class of conflict and consistency problems. The cost is electing, monitoring, and replacing it — but it's usually worth it, which is why replication, Raft, and Kafka all use leaders.

questions.question: What are ZooKeeper and etcd best used for?
options: Storing bulk application data like user records
Small, critical, must-be-consistent facts: leader election, locks, membership, configuration
Caching large media files
High-volume write workloads
questions.explanation: They're small, CP, consensus-backed stores. Because every write needs a majority, they're slow for bulk data but ideal for the few critical, low-volume facts the whole cluster must agree on — leadership, locks, who's alive, consistent config.

questions.question: How does a lease-based distributed lock prevent a crashed holder from blocking everyone forever?
options: The lock never expires, so it can't block
The lock auto-expires after a timeout, so if the holder crashes the lock is released automatically
Other nodes forcibly kill the holder
Locks aren't used in distributed systems
questions.explanation: A lease is a lock with a timeout. If the holder crashes or its session ends (e.g. an ephemeral node vanishes), the lease expires and another node can acquire the lock — so a dead holder never keeps it forever. The tradeoff is the risk of a paused holder overrunning its lease.

questions.question: What problem does a fencing token solve?
options: It encrypts the lock
A paused/old lock holder that wakes up after losing the lock is prevented from corrupting the resource, because the resource rejects its lower (stale) token
It speeds up lock acquisition
It compresses the coordination data
questions.explanation: Each lock grant carries a monotonically increasing token. The protected resource records the highest token it has seen and rejects any operation with a lower one. So a zombie holder that wakes with token 33 after a new holder used 34 has its writes refused — fencing off split-brain at the resource.

questions.question: Why is using a plain Redis key or a database row as a distributed lock risky?
options: It's too slow to be useful
Under a partition or a paused process it can allow two holders (split-brain) and silent corruption, because it lacks consensus-backed grants and fencing
Redis cannot store keys
Database rows can't be deleted
questions.explanation: Naive locks work until a partition or GC pause, when two clients can both believe they hold the lock — the exact failure locks should prevent. Correct distributed locking needs consensus-backed grants plus fencing tokens; rolling your own typically misses the fencing and edge cases.
